name: fp-ts-pragmatic
description: A practical, jargon-free guide to fp-ts functional programming - the 80/20 approach that gets results without the academic overhead. Use when writing TypeScript with fp-ts library.
capabilities: Explains pipe for chaining operations in readable order
Teaches Option type for handling nullable values without null checks
Demonstrates Either for explicit error handling
Shows map and flatMap for transforming and chaining operations
Provides refactoring examples from imperative to functional style
Guides on when NOT to use functional programming for better readability
author: sickn33
securitySummary: This skill is documentation-only (SKILL.md) teaching fp-ts functional programming patterns. All 86 static findings are false positives - the scanner incorrectly treated Markdown code examples as executable code. Backticks are documentation delimiters, not shell commands. No actual network calls, external commands, or cryptographic operations exist in this skill.
valueStatement: Learn functional programming in TypeScript without the academic overhead. This guide focuses on the 20% of fp-ts patterns that deliver 80% of real-world value.
userTitle: Master fp-ts with practical functional programming patterns
limitations: Documentation-only skill - does not execute code or provide runtime assistance
Focuses on fp-ts library patterns, not general functional programming theory
Does not cover advanced topics like monads, functors, or category theory

useCases.title: TypeScript developer adopting fp-ts
useCases.description: Learn practical fp-ts patterns for handling errors, nullable values, and async operations without diving into academic functional programming concepts.

useCases.title: Code refactoring assistance
useCases.description: Get guidance on refactoring imperative code with nested callbacks and null checks into clean functional pipelines using pipe, Option, and Either.

useCases.title: Team onboarding to functional patterns
useCases.description: Use the pragmatic examples and readability guidelines to introduce functional programming concepts to teams without overwhelming them with jargon.

promptTemplates.title: Convert null checks to Option
promptTemplates.prompt: I have this code with nested null checks. Show me how to refactor it using fp-ts Option type: [paste code]
promptTemplates.scenario: Beginner - converting existing null handling to functional style

promptTemplates.title: Handle async errors with TaskEither
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to chain these async operations that might fail. Show me how to use fp-ts TaskEither to handle errors without try-catch: [describe operations]

promptTemplates.title: Refactor callback hell to pipe
promptTemplates.prompt: This code has deep callback nesting. Refactor it using pipe and fp-ts patterns to improve readability: [paste code]

promptTemplates.title: Build validation pipeline
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a validation function using fp-ts Either that collects all validation errors for this data structure: [describe data and rules]

bestPractices: Use pipe when you have 3+ transformations - it improves readability by showing data flow in order
Prefer Either over throwing exceptions for expected failures - it makes error handling explicit in types
Choose readability over cleverness - if a junior developer cannot understand the code, use simpler patterns
antiPatterns: Using fp-ts for simple null checks when optional chaining (?.) works - adds unnecessary complexity
Creating deeply nested functional compositions that are harder to read than imperative code
Introducing fp-ts to teams without functional programming experience without proper onboarding

faq.question: Do I need to know category theory to use fp-ts?
faq.answer: No. This guide focuses on practical patterns without academic jargon. You can use Option, Either, and pipe effectively without understanding monads or functors.

faq.question: When should I NOT use fp-ts?
faq.answer: Skip fp-ts for simple null checks (use optional chaining), performance-critical code (imperative is faster), or when your team lacks FP experience and onboarding is not feasible.

faq.question: Is fp-ts compatible with existing TypeScript code?
faq.answer: Yes. You can adopt fp-ts incrementally. Start with Option for nullable values and Either for error handling, then expand usage as your team becomes comfortable.

faq.question: What is the difference between map and flatMap?
faq.answer: Use map to transform a value inside a container. Use flatMap when the transformation itself might fail and returns the same container type.

faq.question: Does fp-ts work with async/await?
faq.answer: Yes. TaskEither handles async operations that might fail. It wraps Promises and integrates with Either for consistent error handling.

faq.question: How do I handle multiple validation errors?
faq.answer: Use Applicative validation (not covered in basics) to collect all errors instead of stopping at the first failure. This is an advanced topic to explore after mastering Either.
